questions.question: Current flows through a straight solid conductor. Moving from the conductor's centerline outward, the magnetic field strength:
options: Is maximum at the center and falls to zero at the surface
Is uniform across the cross-section and in the surrounding air
Rises from zero at the center to a maximum at the surface, then falls off outside
Is zero everywhere inside the conductor
questions.explanation: Inside a current-carrying conductor the field grows from zero at the center to its peak at the surface; outside, it weakens with distance - the reason circular magnetization concentrates inspection power at the part surface.

questions.question: A stack of steel washers needs inspection for cracks radiating in various directions, with no contact damage allowed. The most efficient circular-field setup is:
options: Prods on each washer individually
All washers threaded onto one central conductor bar
A head shot on each washer between contact plates
A permanent magnet swept across the stack
questions.explanation: A central conductor magnetizes a whole stack of ring-shaped parts at once with no electrical contact and no arc-burn risk - the standard batch technique for washers, nuts, and rings.

questions.question: Prods are set 6 inches apart on a casting. Using typical procedure values of 90-125 amperes per inch of spacing, the current window is roughly:
options: 90 to 125 amperes
270 to 375 amperes
540 to 750 amperes
1,080 to 1,500 amperes
questions.explanation: Six inches at 90-125 A per inch gives about 540-750 amperes. Remember many aerospace and finished-part specifications prohibit prods entirely because of arc-strike risk.

questions.question: After a coil shot, an inspector sees heavy, fuzzy particle accumulation at both extreme ends of a shaft. This is most likely:
options: Two transverse cracks at the ends
Leakage at the magnetic poles formed where flux exits the part - nonrelevant
Proof the amperage was too low
Contamination of the particle bath
questions.explanation: Longitudinal flux must leave and re-enter the part at its ends, creating poles that attract particles. End buildup is expected pole leakage, not a discontinuity indication.

questions.question: A bar 30 inches long and 2 inches in diameter is coil-shot at low fill factor. Which statement reflects correct practice?
options: L/D = 15, so use NI of about 3,000 ampere-turns and magnetize in overlapping sections about 18 inches long
L/D = 15, so one shot at the center covers the whole bar
L/D is too high for a coil - switch to prods
Use 45,000 ampere-turns regardless of geometry
questions.explanation: L/D = 30/2 = 15 (the top of the rule's effective range), giving NI = 45,000/15 = 3,000 ampere-turns - and since adequate field extends a limited distance from the coil, a 30-inch part is magnetized in roughly 18-inch overlapping sections.

questions.question: Why does a pocket field indicator respond to a part after a coil shot but stay silent after a head shot of equal strength?
options: Coil shots use more amperage
Longitudinal fields create external poles; a true circular field is contained within the part
Head shots demagnetize the part automatically
The indicator only works on hollow parts
questions.explanation: A coil-magnetized part has poles at its ends with external leakage the indicator can sense; circular flux closes entirely inside the part, leaving nothing external to detect - which is why circular fields are verified with shims, pie gauges, or a gaussmeter.

questions.question: The skin effect matters in MT because it explains why:
options: AC concentrates the magnetic field at the part surface
DC cannot magnetize steel
Wet particles outperform dry powder
Permanent magnets lose strength with age
questions.explanation: The constant reversal of alternating current confines flux to the outer skin of the part - the source of AC's excellent surface-crack sensitivity, its particle mobility, and its blindness to subsurface flaws.

questions.question: A DC yoke is checked before use at the pole spacing to be used. Per common practice it must lift at least:
options: 10 pounds
25 pounds
40 pounds
100 pounds
questions.explanation: Practices such as ASTM E709/E1444 call for about a 40-pound lift for DC and permanent magnet yokes, versus about 10 pounds for AC yokes, at the working pole spacing.

questions.question: A connecting rod may contain cracks of unknown orientation. The minimum complete magnetization plan is:
options: One high-amperage circular shot - strong fields find everything
Two magnetizations roughly perpendicular to each other, each verified
One longitudinal shot with AC and one with DC in the same direction
Visual inspection first; MT only if something is seen
questions.explanation: Flux only reveals flaws it crosses at about 45-90 degrees, so unknown orientations demand two roughly perpendicular fields - typically one circular and one longitudinal shot, each proven with a shim or gauge.

questions.question: An inspector lays a segmented octagon-shaped gauge on a weld while applying particles with the yoke energized. The particle lines that form across its joints tell the inspector:
options: The exact depth of any subsurface flaw
The approximate field direction and a rough indication of usable strength
The part's permeability in henries per meter
The residual field in gauss after demagnetization
questions.explanation: The pie gauge's segment joints act as artificial flaws: the particle pattern shows which way the field runs and whether it is roughly adequate - a quick directional check, not a calibrated measurement like a Hall-effect gaussmeter reading.
